narrative:
Pleasant but breezy weather today gives way to increasing clouds tonight with a few showers possible late, especially west. Those showers could linger into early Wednesday morning. Scattered showers and a few thundershowers develop in the afternoon. Showers and thundershowers become widespread Wednesday night and continue into Thursday morning as a cold front moves through. Showers taper off Thursday afternoon, but much cooler air arrives Thursday night into Friday morning.

It's the May the Fourth Be With You edition of the Monday Message, now available to our subscribers.

2027 Blue Ridge Calendar Photo Contest

Voting in the 2027 Blue Ridge Calendar Photo Contest ended Sunday. We will announce the winners by the end of this week. Many thanks to everyone who submitted photos and voted in the contest. It's going to be another awesome calendar!

Get the free RWC Mobile App...: Apple and Android

Subscribe Forecast Discussion A large, slow-moving storm system sits over the central and eastern U.S. this week, with high pressure in the Western Atlantic. It shifts east and gradually weakens late in the week. The big story is the swing in temperatures—warmer today and Wednesday, then a turn colder by Thursday night and Friday—with a round of showers and thundershowers Wednesday into Thursday morning as a cold front moves through. Tuesday has pleasant temperatures but breezy southerly winds around high pressure centered to our east and a front approaching from the west. Showers are possible late tonight into early Wednesday, especially on the west side of the Appalachians. Scattered showers with a few thundershowers develop during the afternoon. Showers and thundershowers become widespread Wednesday night. This is the main period of rain. The front crosses the Southern Appalachians west to east early Thursday morning. Showers remain possible Thursday morning, then taper off by afternoon and linger along the western escarpment. Light showers end even there early Thursday evening. High pressure is centered overhead Friday with clearing skies and cooler temperatures. A dry front moves through Saturday with little impact. Sunday is quiet before widely scattered showers, and possibly a thundershower, arrive Sunday night into early Monday. May 04, 2026 May 2026 Weather Outlook Monthly Forecast Forecast for the Southern Appalachians – SW Virginia & Western North Carolina The May Pattern: Transition Month May is a transition month across the Southern Appalachians. The trees become fully green even at the highest elevations, and bloom season is on full display. Frost potential goes almost to nil by the end of the month, even in the most frost-prone mountain valleys. Climatologically, it’s one of the wetter months of the year, often featuring a battle between lingering cool air masses and increasing warmth and humidity from the south. That clash can produce frequent showers and thunderstorms, along with some of the more active weather of the spring season. Bottom Line for May 2026 April ran warmer and much drier than average, but the end of April took a much cooler turn. That late-April cooldown marks the start of two weeks of cooler-than-average weather in May. But the last two weeks of May will be warmer than average, leading to a draw for the month overall. Expect significant variation, averaging out to near-normal for the month. For precipitation, we start with a relatively dry week, followed by near-average precipitation for the rest of the month, resulting in slightly drier-than-average conditions overall. Temperatures: Chilly Start, Warm Finish The extended-range guidance from the ECMWF, GFS, and Canadian models is in decent agreement on the overall temperature pattern. An eastern trough persists over the eastern United States, supporting cooler-than-average temperatures through mid-May. The transition to this pattern is already underway in the final days of April. In week three of May, the trough weakens and the pattern gradually transitions toward a more zonal flow. By the last week of May, a broader ridge develops across much of the country, leading to a warming trend. See the graphic below showing the dramatically different 500mb height anomalies for the beginning vs. end of May. So, we’ll have cooler temperatures in the first half of the month and warmer temperatures in the second half. This balances out to near-average temperatures for the month overall. Precipitation: Early Deficit, Limited Recovery The precipitation pattern is less well defined but follows a similar evolution. Early in the month, the same trough that brings cooler weather does not favor much rainfall (although there will be more rain to the south—in Asheville and Hickory—than to the north in Boone and SW VA). The precipitation signal for the final three weeks of May is somewhat wetter, but not dramatically so. We are not expecting intense or prolonged precipitation events. Rainfall during this time will come from weak fronts moving through the zonal flow and, by week four, from “hit-or-miss” afternoon and nighttime thundershowers. The net effect is slightly drier-than-average conditions for the month. May Outlook at a Glance
Week Temperature Precipitation
Week 1 (May 1–7) Cooler than average Drier than average
Week 2 (May 8–14) Cooler than average Near average
Week 3 (May 15–21) Trending warmer Near average
Week 4 (May 22–31) Above average Near average
Overall for May Below average Slightly below average
Factors Considered in this Forecast • The AO, NAO, and PNA indices strongly indicate the cooler start in the first week to 10 days of May, followed by a return to near-normal temperatures around mid-month or slightly later. AO (Arctic Oscillation) and NAO (North Atlantic Oscillation): Both indices take a sharp dip in the first 10 days of May, then gradually trend back toward neutral. PNA (Pacific–North American): Strongly positive early in the month, then trending toward neutral. • ENSO: For context, La Niña is gone. The central Pacific is now officially ENSO-neutral but trending warmer. There is a reasonable chance we are looking at a budding El Niño late in the month. However, May’s weather has little direct correlation with the El Niño–Southern Oscillation. That said, El Niño appears to be developing and could carry into next winter—that will be interesting. A trend toward El Niño also usually suppresses tropical activity in the Atlantic as we move into hurricane season. • Extended ECMWF, GFS, and Canadian modeling: All three models are in good agreement with the overall pattern for the month but differ somewhat on the timing and intensity of the temperature rebound in the last two weeks. The Canadian is slightly warmer and drier than the other two models. Climatological Average for May

Apr 30, 2026 Breaking Down The Drought One of the most (if not the most) prevalent topics of the last month on RaysWeather.Com has been the ongoing drought that is exhibiting a tight grip on our region. With how much we have been mentioning it in our forecast discussions and videos, we figured it is about time that we do a deeper dive into the impacts and the numbers associated with the drought as we wrap up April. How Dry Is It? Well, pretty darn dry. The Hickory airport experienced it's driest six month period from October 25th to April 25th in the 77-year history at the site, with just 12.32 inches of rain. That's over 10 inches less than is normal for that period of time! Over that same six month period, it has been: 4th driest in Boone Driest on record at Beech Mountain Driest on record in Celo 2nd driest in Hot Springs Driest on record in Galax Driest on record in Old Fort Driest on record in Brevard 8th driest in Asheville 15th driest in Wytheville Now, this is for a somewhat arbitrary period of time, but it illustrates the point well. Many locations locally experienced their combined driest end of fall, winter, and start of spring on record. The spring season to date has been exceptionally warm and dry as well. Through that same date of April 25th, 9 local reporting sites have logged their warmest spring on record (so far): Boone, Beech Mountain, Hickory, Asheville, Old Fort, Celo, Hot Springs, Galax, and Mountain City, TN. Mean temperatures are running anywhere from 5 to 8 degrees above normal, and are being boosted greatly by how warm our afternoons have been. Afternoon high temperatures are running 8 to 10 degrees above normal, while morning lows sit 4 to 6 degrees above normal. So, What Does That Mean? For many of us that are not farmers, it can be a little harder to see what the impacts of a drought are beyond the lowered lakes and rivers around here. Most of the region is outlined in the level 3 "extreme drought," which is the lighter red coloring on the cover image, and has been for multiple weeks. This is indeed manifesting in more than just lakes running below full pool: the towns of Morganton and North Wilkesboro have sought voluntary water conservation from citizens. No enforcement of any restrictions, but being proactive in the event the drought persists and/or worsens. Caldwell County has put in place minor water restrictions, and local farmers have reported that they are having to cut crops. If the drought should persist, harvests later this year will not be what they usually are, and further water restrictions may be necessary for counties and municipalities. Burn bans in North Carolina and Virginia may be extended further into the end of spring and start of summer, and wildfire danger will stay elevated. The National Park Service is beginning a project this summer to remove Helene debris from 3,000 acres of Blue Ridge Parkway-adjacent land in order to mitigate the wildfire risk, as the volume of downed trees and limbs can serve as a timber box for any fire that sparks. Will There Ever Be Any Relief? As we wrap up the month of April and creep closer to summer in the southeast, the "method" for rain begins to shift. Over the fall, winter, and most of spring, most of our rain comes from individual low pressure systems that cut through the region and provide long-lasting, soaking rains. As the calendar flips to May, the transition to summertime pop-up thunderstorms is in full swing, bringing plenty of days where some folks recieve an inch or two of rain while others are left high and dry. We have recieved two beneficial rainfall events in a three-day stretch as of this writing, one on Saturday 4/25 and one on Tuesday 4/28. Several stations across the mountains recorded over an inch of rain from these two events combined. That is helpful, but it is far from a drought-ending event. In fact, with a drought this prolonged, there is almost certainly no one event that would end the drought. Many areas are 8 to 12 inches behind schedule over the past six months, and it will take several soaking rains to catch up to where we should be heading into the summer. Check out Rosman, down in the bottom left corner in the wettest area in the Eastern US: almost 20 inches below normal for the last six months! It will likely be difficult to get rid of the drought over the course of the summer, simply from the nature of summertime precipitation being generally more scattered. An 8 to 12 inch deficit is not one that is quickly erased. It will take months of average to above average precipitation to clear out that deficit, but the short-term goal is simply to get out of the more severe drought that we are currently in, which can be accomplished over a shorter period of time with some cooperation from the weather. There is reason for optimism, with a more active pattern finally settling in to end April and begin May after weeks of dry sunshine, as well as burgeoning El Nino conditions developing through fall and winter, which tends to lead to more opportunities for precipitation. Even so, at least some level of drought is likely to continue for several months. Apr 28, 2026 See all Golf-O-Meter 0 Golfballs Only ducks or polar bears. Good day for knitting. Maybe, if you are brave. Worth taking a chance. A good day to be outside. Bosses, you'd better stand at the door. (Or will bosses be first out the door?)
